THE WHITE HOUSE ~EeRfl'f WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: President's Meeting with Prime Minister Benazir Bhutto of Pakistan (7) PARTICIPANTS: U.S. The President James Baker III, Secretary of State John Sununu, Chief of Staff Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Marlin Fitzwater, Assistant to the President for Press Relations James A. Kelly, NSC, Notetaker Pakistan Prime Minister Benazir Bhutto Yaqub Khan, Foreign Minister Egbal Akhund, Adviser on Foreign Affairs General N. Barbar, Presidential Adviser Khalid Maholud, Notetaker DATE, TIME February 24, 1989, 4:51-5:22 p.m. AND PLACE: American Ambassador's residence, Tokyo, Japan President Bush welcomed the Prime Minister, noting that it was a new beginning for each country. (U) Prime Minister Bhutto offered her thanks and immediately began serious discussion. She said there was one area which was complicating relations between the countries very seriously. She wanted to start with this critical topic, the nuclear issue. The Prime Minister referred to an earlier letter from President Ronald Reagan in which the President said he had been following certain trends and that if these trends in Pakistan were not restrained it would not be possible to certify the country under American laws involving nuclear matters. Kelly, NSC, Notetaker Nigeria President Ibrahim Babangida Ike Nwachukwu, Minister of External Affairs Several others DATE, TIME February 25, 1989, 11:50 a.m. - 12:09 p.m. AND PLACE: u.S. Ambassador's residence, Tokyo, Japan President Bush, after greeting the President with warmth, commented on a pick-up in oil prices of the day before. He again thanked President Babangida for corning to call, noting that Japanese security had made moving about town difficult for him. After President Babangida responded by congratulating President Bush on his election, the President pointed out that he was an American president who is very interested in Africa. He has visited many African countries and hopes to visit more. America will not neglect her friends, the Pr~sident said. President Babangida expressed a growing optimism. T E H C USE WAS...,;:--'G70N MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Salinas PARTICIPANTS: The President President Salinas of Mexico DATE, TIME March 10, 1989; 1:49-1:53 p.m.; AND PLACE: Mexico City to Oval Office President Salinas of Mexico telephoned the President at 1:49 pm on March 10, 1989. President Salinas congratulated the President for Secretary Brady's announcement on the debt. The President asked whether he had read it. Salinas answered affirmatively stating that he considered it anew and most important approach. The President said the issue had been a priority one and that he would now have to follow-up on it. President Salinas noted the Mexican Government would strongly endorse the announcement today, saying he is convinced the President is prepared to respond to the problem. The President said he hoped the Mexico endorsement will have good reverberations in Soutl1.America. President Salinas stated his belief that the announcement will bring results. He stated he wanted to make one comment: that it is fundamental to make the proposal front-loaded. He noted front-loading is essential to debt reduction and capital repatriation. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: SE6REr THE WHITE HOUSE ...SECRill' WASHINGTON 1781 MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Meeting with Prime Minister Manley of Jamaica PARTICIPANTS: The President The Vice President Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Michael Sotirhos, U.S. Ambassador to Jamaica Richard H. Melton, Deputy Assistant Secretary of State Robert S. Pastorino (NSC Notetaker) Michael Manley, Prime Minister of Jamaica Keith Johnson, Jamaican Ambassador David Coore, Foreign Minister Fred Francis, Foreign Ministry William Byrce, Foreign Ministry DATE, TIME March 15, 1989, 3:55 p.m. - 4:10 p.m. AND PLACE: Oval Office ( ) The President began the meeting by congratulating Prime Minister Manley on his electoral victory, reminding Manley they had previously met on several social occasions. Prime Minister Manley noted his Government wants the best possible relations with the U.S. He said he would work very hard toward this end and noted several positive changes had taken place in the past year: an improved investment climate and a rising "Manley stock market". He concluded by saying the U.S. would be the center of Jamaican international relations. The Prime Minister noted he had talked to Bill Bennett and Jack Lawn about plans to continue the fight against drugs; he noted that a common intelligence network is needed. Pastorino, NSC (Notetaker) Fernando Solana, Mexican Secretary of Foreign Affairs Gustavo Petricioli, Mexican Ambassador to the United States DATE, TIME March 20, 1989, 2:32 - 2:50 p.m. AND PLACE: Oval Office The President began the meeting by telling Solana that the U.S. wanted President Salinas to learn that he was welcome to pay a '") State Visit to Washington at any time. He noted that it would be appropriate to have Salinas as the first visitor. But he said it was up to Salinas to pick the most convenient time; he said the U.S. is sensitive to Salinas' needs and schedule. Foreion Secretarv Solana noted that President Salinas very much wants to visit Washington but a visit a little later in the year would be more convenient -- perhaps in September or October. Solana noted Salinas' desire for the best possible U.S. - Mexican relations. He added that a bilateral commission might take place before the State Visit. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: .c;oUF I J;)6~IT H'LL -CONFIDENTIAL 2900 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHI NGTON DECLASSIFIED PER E.O. 12958, AS AMENDED (ilO(U) - 0'1,2 '_I=' MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION ;}1- ~/ ZI/tJ, SUBJECT: The President's Telephone Conversation with President Barco of Colombia PARTICIPANTS: The President David Pacelli, NSC (Notetaker) President Barco DATE, TIME April 14, 1989, 4:56 p.m. - 4:58 p.m. AND PLACE: White House Tennis Court - VIP Lounge, Andrews Air Force Base President Barco, speaking in English, said hello. The President said, "Hello, Mr. President." President Barco said, "How are you?" The President said he was good. He stated that he had not expected to be in town today, but that he "had a deal" with the U.S. Senate, so he had to come back. President Barco said he was looking forward to seeing the President. The President said that he had heard that President Barco had given a speech and that it was "a hard hitting speech." "Everyone was talking about it." The President said he had "great identification with the problems you have down there" with drugs. He added that-he had high regard for Colombia's anti- narcotics efforts. The President said that we made an announcement about President Barco's visit in July and that the staffs of the two countries would arrange a specific date. He stated that he was looking forward to seeing President Barco and discussing topics of common interest. Pr

The Prime Minister expressed his condolences over the loss of life from the explosion on the battleship ~. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: $'8HFIDEN'fIAL £ONFIDENTIAt 3784 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHl NGTON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conver~ation with President Arias of Costa Rica PARTICIPANTS: The President Notetaker: David Pacelli, NSC Notetaker: Susan Bunch, NSC President Arias DATE, TIME April 28, 1989, 5:21 - 5:33 P.M. AND PLACE: Oval Office - Presidential Office President Arias was called by President Bush. The President and Arias greeted each other and began the conversation by asking how each other was. The President said he was calling to let Arias know that he appreciated his speaking out on the Sandinista electoral law and media reform law. Jim Baker told me of your outstanding efforts in contacting foreign leaders, the President stated, adding that it was wonderful and he appreciated it very much. (¢) President Arias said that the President didn't have to thank him for that. It is my strong belief, he said, that the Sandinistas are not complying with what they have promised. He had been in touch with Carlos Andres Perez, the Germans, Norwegians, and Swedes, adding, "As you know, the Nordic governments are very difficult. They don't seem to care about democracy in this part of the world." Arias said he had no problem with the rest of the countries. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: SEGREt THE WHITE HOUSE SECRE'f WASHINGTON 4791 MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Meeting with Datuk Seri Dr. Mahathir bin Mohamad, Prime Minister of Malaysia, Kuala Lumpur (~) PARTICIPANTS: The President Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs DATE, TIME May 20, 1989 AND PLACE: Boston, Massachusetts Prime Minister Mahathir: The Prime Minister raised the Cambodia issue and opined that the Khmer Rouge, left alone, could win out. (1) The President: The President asked what might happen in China. (~) Prime Minister Mahathir: The Prime Minister indicated that events might turn out like Burma if the authorities bring in troops from outside. However, they must worry about Hong Kong, Taiwan, etc. The students are trying to force the pace and this is not wise. The students should move more gradually and restrain themselves. China is not ready yet for elections. ($) The President: The President characterized U.S.-Malaysian relations as being OK. Although it sometimes seems that they are swallowed up by the Cambodian problem and attention to ASEAN, the President declared that he did not want to neglect our bilateral relationship. The President raised the Uruguay Round indicating that it was the best way to work out trade problems. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: 6E6RE~ 5724 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHI NGTON DECLASSIFIED PER E.O. 12958, AS AMENDED doooo-oLfd.Cf-F MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION JL 813 1/oq SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Arias of Costa Rica PARTICIPANTS: The President Oscar Sanchez Arias, President of the Republic of Costa Rica Notetaker: David A. Pacelli Stenographer: Pat Battenfield DATE, TIME July 20, 1989, 11:05 to 11:14 EDT AND PLACE: The Oval Office - Arias Residence The President, who initiated the call, began by saying, "Mr. President, how are you today?" (U) President Arias replied, "Fine, thank you." (U) The President said that he had told Arias that he would get back to him after an earlier phone call. The President asked Arias to pardon the delay, saying that he had just returned from Europe. The President said he was calling to simply say that he was very pleased to be able to accept Arias's invitation to come to San Jose in October to celebrate 100 years of Costa Rican democracy. The President congratulated Arias on this initiative, saying there is no better place than Costa Rica to have a hemispheric meeting, particularly given its tradition of democracy. The President said that he had given the invitation some thought and had checked the schedule. pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: The President Carlos Salinas de Gortari, President of Mexico Notetaker: William T. Pryce
pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME July 24, 1989, 2:49 - 2:54 pm EST AND PLACE: Oval Office
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: ..SECRET 5897 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHI NGTON DECLASSIFIED PER EaO~ 12958, AS AMENDED ~a,,~ - o'/;). CJ - F MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION 1'- 8!ZI}IJ <'J SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Carlos Salinas de Gortari of Mexico PARTICIPANTS: The President Carlos Salinas de Gortari, President of Mexico Notetaker: William T. Pryce DATE, TIME July 24, 1989, 2:49 - 2:54 pm EST AND PLACE: Oval Office President Salinas greeted the President in English with "Hello Mr. President, this is Carlos Salinas de Gortari." (U) The President acknowledged by affirming "This is George Bush." (U) President Salinas stated that he wanted to call to share with the President the satisfaction he and the Mexican Government felt with the finalization of the Debt Agreement. (I) The President responded that he was very pleased to hear of President Salinas' satisfaction; that Mexico had come a long way and that President Salinas had his respect for the way he had approached the situation. The President expressed his delight in knowing that President Salinas was in a position to express his satisfaction with the way things worked out. vt> President Salinas replied that he wanted to express his personal recognition and thanks for the unfailing support that the President and United States Government officials had given in this matter. He mentioned that Secretary Brady had been especially helpful.

pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: ffitflBENllAL POINTS TO BE MADE FOR TELEPHONE CALL TO PRIME MINISTER THATCHER I just received your letter on assistance to Poland. I welcome your views and will reply soon on this issue. However, today I would like to talk to you about the situation in Colombia. As you know, the situation in Colombia is a serious concern for us. The traffickers there have sought to undermine the legal and social fabric of that nation by threatening and killing Vi judges, corrupting and intimidating the police, the military and other institutions. The attack on one of the leading ~wspapers (El Espectador) over the weekend is but one sickening example. -- ~herefore very much appreciated your timely letter last ~ week on your readiness to join in supporting Colombia. President Barco has sho~ great courage in deciding to stand up to the cartel~ave spoken with President Barco several times, and we intend to give him all the support .F9.$...~i:t:51e. vr£e $ 65 million in emergency military aid which I offered two weeks ago was but a first step. Tonight I will -- announce a $260 million program for Colombia, Peru, and Bolivia for next year and a $2 billion program over the next l~iJTe years. pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME August 21, 1989, 8:04 - 8:20 p.m. AND PLACE: Kennebunkport, Maine
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHl NGTON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with Virgilio Barco Vargas, President of Colombia . PARTICIPANTS: The President Virgilio Barco Vargas Notetaker: William T. Pryce DATE, TIME August 21, 1989, 8:04 - 8:20 p.m. AND PLACE: Kennebunkport, Maine The President: Mr. President, I am calling to express my condolences on behalf of everybody in the U.S. on the loss of Presidential candidate Galan. It is a terrible thing and we are thinking of you and your courage. I want to work with you to ensure that these Colombians and those before have not died in vain. I want to tell you that we stand ready to help in any responsible way you feel appropriate. It is strictly your call as President of a friendly country. We are ready to respond as quickly as possible to support your police and military in the execution of your new decree laws. I want you to know you have my strong support and admiration for the courageous and sweeping decrees you have adopted. Frankly, from our standpoint, not only is reinstitution of extradition an important move, but provision for the seizure of traffickers' property is critical. And again,. if you request it, and only if you request it, we will try to provide assistance to protect judges and other officials who are threatened. (t) President Barco: . pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: SEGRE:r 6857 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHI NGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Francois Mitterrand of France (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President President Mitterrand Interpreter: Alec Toumayan Notetaker: Adrian Basora, NSC DATE, TIME August 26, 1989, 10:33 - 10:45 a.m. EDT AND PLACE: Kennebunkport, Maine The President opened by saying he was in Kennebunkport and had thought about President Mitterrand. (U) President Mitterrand said Kennebunkport brought back pleasant memories. (U) The President said he wanted President Mitterrand to know that he understood what Mitterrand was doing on Lebanon and that Mitterrand had the President's strong support. ~) President Mitterrand said he had taken good note of the President's statement and appreciated it very much; it contributed to the French position. President Mitterrand stressed that the French position was not an aggressive one. France was prepared for the very worst while hoping for the very best. President Mitterrand asked if the President had any suggestions. </) The President said he was very concerned about what more could be done. He agreed a ceasefire was needed and that the pressure should be kept on Assad and Aoun. Obviously there also had to be internal reforms and the withdrawal of foreign forces. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: " CQW~IQENTIM 6814 MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with Virgilio Barco Vargas, President of Colombia PARTICIPANTS: The President Virgilio Barco Vargas Note Taker: William T. Pryce DATE, TIME August 28, 1989,- 3:52 - 4:00 p.m. AND PLACE: Kennebunkport, Maine President Barco called The President to thank him for our help with Colombia's narcotics problems. President Barco: I just wanted to call you to thank you for all your help. The President: You are very nice to call and I hope we can be of help now and in the future. President Barco: I want to inform you that we are making a number of further arrests of cartel members. The President: I am delighted to hear that. I know that it has been a tremendous strain for you and your government and we admire you. We don't want to push in any direction you are not comfortable with. We are here to help you. President Barco: We are doing everything that is possible. I wanted to let you know there are two issues where you were especially helpful. One is the troop issue and your personal comments were extremely helpful. I know that the Attorney General's statement wa's taken out of context. The second is I understand the Bennett Plan emphasizes consumers which is right. As you know, this is the only way to end the drug problem and I must congratulate Mr. Bennett for his plan. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: 7429 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHI NGTON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with Margaret Thatcher of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland (I) PARTICIPANTS: The President Margaret Thatcher, Prime Minister of Great Britain Notetaker: R. Rand Beers DATE, TIME September 5, 1989, 1:15 P.M. - 1:30 P.M. AND PLACE: White Hous~ Situation Room The President telephoned Prime Minister Thatcher concerning the drug crisis. (1'> The President greeted PM Thatcher, "Margaret?" (~) PM Thatcher replied, "Hello, George, how are you? You have an official speech tonight, don't you?" (I) The President responded, "I'm just fine. I feel like a Spring colt, I'm just back from vacation. Yes, I was calling you on that subject (speech), if you have a minute. I got your letter on assistance to Poland and I'm very pleased to have your views. But I am calling on Colombia. The traffickers there have sought to undermine the whole social fabric of that nation. The attack on the EI Espectador over the weekend is one example. Barco is facing a terrible situation. I appreciated your letter on Colombia telling me of your support. I have talked to Barco several times. I admire the position Barco is taking. We intend to give all the suppor,t we possibly can." (~ PM Thatcher said, "I think your own action was exactly right." (1) DECLASSIFIED QOHFIDE1J'fIAb PER E.O. 12958,

pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: f BECRE'i'''" SE6REf 7293 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHI NGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Meeting with Uruguayan President Julio Maria Sanguinetti (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President Lawrence Eagleburger, Deputy Secretary of State Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Robert M. Gates, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Everett Ellis Briggs, Senior Director, Latin America and the Caribbean Michael Skol, Deputy Assistant Secretary of State for Inter-American Affairs Julio Maria Sanguinetti, President of Uruguay Luis Angel Barrios Tassano, Minister of Foreign Affairs Ricardo Zerbino Cavajani, Minister of Economy and Finance Hector Luisi, Ambassador to the United States DATE, TIME September 15, 1989, 3:05 - 3:50 p.m. EST AND PLACE: The Oval Office The President and President Sanguinetti met for 45 minutes in the Oval Office on September 15, 1989. (U) The President, after an exchange of pleasantries during which the American and Uruguayan media representatives were allowed to be present, referred to the visit President Sanguinetti had just made to Japan. The President said he had noticed a significant difference between the" new Japanese government and the Socialists, not only on defense policy but on trade issues. pdfExtract.subject: SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Virgilio Barco of Colombia
pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: The President President Virgilio Barco of Colombia Notetaker: Everett Briggs
pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME September 26, 1989, 8:45 - 8:52 a.m. EST AND PLACE: Washington/Bogota
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: 7617 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHI NGTON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Virgilio Barco of Colombia PARTICIPANTS: The President President Virgilio Barco of Colombia Notetaker: Everett Briggs DATE, TIME September 26, 1989, 8:45 - 8:52 a.m. EST AND PLACE: Washington/Bogota The President initiated a telephone call to President Virgilio Barco. The conversation took place in English. The President: How are you this morning? President Barco: I'm glad to hear you. The President: Your embassy here thought we were cut off yesterday. I had finished, but wondered if you had any additional items to raise. President Barco: I'm very interested in accepting a request from several congressmen to meet with them, to support your war on drugs. I don't know if you could help with this. Maybe if the White House showed interest in my having such a meeting, I could do so. I know you are probably busy today. I'd make a 15-minute speech. The President: Which senators? The more contact you have the better. But do you mean a joint session of Congress, or what? President Barco: Yes, but I'm not sure if it is with all of them, or just the leaders. The President: Why don't you let my office know, and we'll be glad to help. President Barco: How is the best way to do it? The President: Through our ambassador there, or through your ambassador here. When you have the

(U) The President and President Barco discussed a recent meeting U.S. Ambassador McNamara conducted with President Barco. (~) The President noted he appreciated President Barco's concerns from U.S. newspapers clippings about the security threat surrounding the summit. The President said he wishes to work out any problems, but that he does have legitimate security concerns and wishes President Barco to discuss these with the head of the U.S. Secret Service. This would enable U.S. security people to share their concerns and provide intelligence reports that the Colombians may not have seen. (1) President Barco noted he and his family often travel to Cartagena and that he believes it is safe there. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: eOHFIDENtpIAL udr~( !DENT IAt 1597 M£MCON THE WHITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Cartagena Drug Summit PARTICIPANTS: President George Bush Secretary James Baker Director Bill Bennett President Virgilio Barco of Colombia President Alan Garcia of Peru President Paz Zamora of Bolivia Foreign Minister Julio Londona of Colombia Minister of Foreign Affairs Carlos Iturralde of Bolivia DATE, TIME February 15, 1990, 11:25 A.M. - 1:35 P.M. AND PLACE: Cartagena, Colombia Notes from the Plenary Session, Cartagena Drug Summit, which included the four presidents. Due to the make-up of the group and the numbers involved, the following notes are more observation than verbatim and therefore not in the first person as is usually the case for MEMCON's. The meeting lasted from 11:25 A.M. to approximately 1:35 P.M. (U) President Barco opened the meeting with a very short statement (5 minutes) in which he basically welcomed everybody to the summit but did not offer any particular substantive observations. (U) President Paz Zamora of Bolivia followed President Barco with a very upbeat statement noting at the outset that it was remarkable that the Declaration of Cartagena had been negotiated in such a short time. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: ..£O~JFIBEN'fIM CONFIDENTIAl: 3346 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHI NGTON DECLASSIFIED PER E.O. 12958, AS AMENDED MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION (}OOIJ F 6'ta., .. r 1'- g/~l/b1 SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Virgilio Barco of Colombia (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President Virgilio Barco Vargas, President of Colombia Notetaker: Donald C. Johnson DATE, TIME April 27, 1990, 11:25 - 11:30 a.m. EST AND PLACE: Oval Office The President telephoned President Barco to express his condolences over the Pizarro killing and to reaffirm our support. (U) The President: Mr. Barco, Mr. President, sir. (U) President Barco: I am very glad to hear you. (U) The President: I'm calling because I wanted to say how outraged we are over the murder of Pizarro. Just when it seems the drug lords have gone to the outer limits of brutality, they carry out some even more terrible act. I just wanted to know that we are thinking of you. We wanted to convey our condolences to Pizarro's family. Obviously we did not share a common idealogy, but Pizarro had renounced violence and was working within the democratic system. And, it was through your leadership that he had been convinced to compete through the polls and not through bullets. We applauded that and I feel very badly for the people of Colombia. (?) President Barco: It was very bad because it was an example of how we had changed. He had many enemies,

pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME June 14, 1990, 11:00 - 12:00pm, One-on-One Meeting AND PLACE: Oval Office
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: SECRET 4500 THE WH ITE HOUSE Add-On WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION MEMCON SUBJECT: Meeting with Chatichai Chunhawan of Thailand ~ PARTICIPANTS: The President Karl D. Jackson, Senior Director for Asian Affairs (Notetaker) Chatichai Chunhawan, Prime Minister Sathianthai Surakait, International Law Advisor to the Prime Minister DATE, TIME June 14, 1990, 11:00 - 12:00pm, One-on-One Meeting AND PLACE: Oval Office The President welcomed the Prime Minister and held a brief meeting in the Oval Office. (U) The President: Welcome, Prime Minister Chatichai. We view this as an important visit pnd are very pleased to have this distinguished visit from the Prime Minister of Thailand. I thought we would spend a minute in here before we move into the Cabinet Room. Afterwards we will go to White House for a working lunch. I am very pleased you are here. (U) On Cambodia, we have worked closely to achieve our immediate strategic objective, the withdrawal of Vietnamese forces. But we continue to worry about a longer term threat of Vietnamese domination and, therefore, a comprehensive political settlement is necessary. We want to see Cambodia as an independent buffer. In the U.S. there is great dread of the Khmer Rouge. Just for working with ASEAN, I am being accused of favoring the Khmer Rouge. (1) We welcome your own commitment to a comprehensive political settlement. We welcome what Thail

pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: The President James A. Baker, Secretary of State Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Bernard Aronson, Assistant Secretary of State for Inter-American Affairs William T. Pryce, Senior Director for Latin American Affairs, NSC (Notetaker) Luis Alberto Lacalle, President Hector Gros-Espiell, Minister of Foreign Affairs Enrique Silva Braga, Minister of Economy and Finance Eduardo MacGillycuddy, Ambassador to the United States
pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME October 1, 1990, 8:30 - 9:00 p.m. EST AND PLACE: Waldorf Astoria Hotel, New York, N.Y.
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: -€OMF IDEH'f lAL " -BONFIOENTIAL 8098 THE WHITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Meeting with President Luis Alberto Lacalle of Uruguay (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President James A. Baker, Secretary of State Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Bernard Aronson, Assistant Secretary of State for Inter-American Affairs William T. Pryce, Senior Director for Latin American Affairs, NSC (Notetaker) Luis Alberto Lacalle, President Hector Gros-Espiell, Minister of Foreign Affairs Enrique Silva Braga, Minister of Economy and Finance Eduardo MacGillycuddy, Ambassador to the United States DATE, TIME October 1, 1990, 8:30 - 9:00 p.m. EST AND PLACE: Waldorf Astoria Hotel, New York, N.Y. President Lacalle: I want to thank you for your interest in our debt program. It appears to be on the right track. yzf The President: Treasury is trying to help. We did talk with Mr. McPherson and he thinks the deal can be done. ~ President Lacalle: Thank you for your intervention. There is more than money involved. The psychological input is important. Thank you very much. We are working currently in Washington on a cooperation treaty. We are also studying things that we could sign when you are there. We hope you can spend the night in Punta del Este. ~ The President: We are working on that. (jI) President Lacalle: We are in a final round of conversations.

pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME November 28, 1990, 2:20 - 2:40 p.m. EST AND PLACE: The Oval Office (The President met with Vice President Arias-Calderon after Arias-Calderon's meeting with Vice President Quayle.) (U)
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: COtllE IDEN IlAL CONFIDENTIAL 9816 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Meeting with Ricardo Arias-Calderon of the Republic of Panama (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President The Vice President Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Bernard W. Aronson, Assistant Secretary of State for Inter-American Affairs William T. Pryce, Senior Director for Latin American Affairs, NSC (Notetaker) Ricardo Arias-Calderon, 1st Vice'President cif Panama Eduardo Vallarino, Panamanian Ambassador to the United States DATE, TIME November 28, 1990, 2:20 - 2:40 p.m. EST AND PLACE: The Oval Office (The President met with Vice President Arias-Calderon after Arias-Calderon's meeting with Vice President Quayle.) (U) The President: We had a very positive visit with Mexican President Salinas. How are things with you and Mexico? ~ Vice President Arias-Calderon: Well, not so good, We still do not have normal relations. (~ The President: Oh, what is the problem? ~) Vice President Arias-Calderon: They have given asylum to the head of the Central Bank who had stolen a great deal of money and is under criminal charges. And it was therefore difficult for us to recognize asylum and allow him to leave the country. We have attempted to say, "Lets put this question aside and move ahead in our bilateral relations," but this has not been possible. ~ The President: It

pdfExtract.subject: SUBJECT: Telcon with President Jorge Serrano of the Republic of Guatemala on June 11, 1991 (U)
pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: The President President Jorge Serrano Notetakers: Bill Pryce/David Pacelli
pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME June 11, 1991, 10:40 - 11:07am EDT AND PLACE: The Oval Office - Presidential Palace
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: .5ECRE':t'· SEGRE+ 4354 THE WHITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telcon with President Jorge Serrano of the Republic of Guatemala on June 11, 1991 (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President President Jorge Serrano Notetakers: Bill Pryce/David Pacelli DATE, TIME June 11, 1991, 10:40 - 11:07am EDT AND PLACE: The Oval Office - Presidential Palace The President returned a phone call from President Serrano which he was unable to take earlier. President Serrano spoke in English. (U) The President: Hello Mr. President, how are you? (U) President Serrano: Hello Mr. President. How are you, sir? I'm just fine, just working away here. (U) The President: All is well here. I am feeling okay. I'm delighted to hear from you. (U) President Serrano: I would like to talk with you on a couple of questions. I've been trying to call you for two weeks. (K) The President: This is a good time to talk. I'd like to know about any problems you might want to bring up with me. (U) President Serrano: I don't know if you remember me. We've met several times. (~) The President: Yes, I think I met you in a meeting when I was down there at one point·. ~) President Serrano: Yes, I also met you in your office with Charlie Bartlett. (U) The President: Charlie Bartlett has told me about you. He's a very strong supporter of yours and I feel like I know you. He went down to your inauguration. (U)

pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: SgGRET SEGRET 4650 NATIONAL SECURITY COUNCIL WASHINGTON. D.C. 20506 MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telcon with Cesar Gaviria Trujillo, President of Colombia, June 20, 1991 PARTICIPANTS: The President Cesar Gaviria Trujillo, President Notetaker: R. Rand Beers DATE, TIME June 20, 1991, 5:13 - 5:18 P.M. EST AND PLACE: The Oval Office The President returned a telephone call from President Gaviria concerning the surrender of Pablo Escobar. President Gaviria spoke in English. (I) The President: Hello, Mr. President. How are you? (U) President Gaviria: Hello, how are you? (U) The President: Just fine. I am delighted to hear from you. (U) President Gaviria: I was calling to tell you about Escobar. vt) The President: We keep on having the line cut out. Can you hear me now? (U) President Gaviria: Yes. (U) The President: Now, that is a little better. (U) President Gaviria: I wanted to tell you about the surrender of Escobar. It was a victory of law and our joint effort. He surrendered yesterday. He was transported by helicopter to the jail. UpOfr arrival, ·the judicial process opened against him. ~ About the jail, we have set it up to avoid him from being able to escape. It is also a big jail. The press has reported that it is a luxurious jail. That is not true. It's not luxurious. You can verify that, it's no better than any other. pdfExtract.declassification: DECLASSIFIED PER E.Os 12958, Declassify on: OADR
pdfExtract.subject: SUBJECT: Meeting with Jean-Bertrand Aristide, President of Haiti (U)
pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: The President The Vice President James A. Baker, Secretary of State John H. Sununu, Chief of Staff Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Bernard W. Aronson* Assistant Secretary of State for Inter-American Affairs William T. Pryce, Senior Director, Latin American Affairs, NSC (Notetaker) Eliza Burham, Interpreter Jean-Bertrand Aristide, President of Haiti Jean Casimir, Ambassador to the United States Fritz Longchamps, Ambassador to the United Nations Raymond Valcin, Minister Counselor, Embassy of Haiti
pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME October 4, 1991, 9:40 - 10:10 a.m., D.S.T. AND PLACE: Oval Office
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: CONFIDENTIAL 7388 THE WHITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Meeting with Jean-Bertrand Aristide, President of Haiti (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President The Vice President James A. Baker, Secretary of State John H. Sununu, Chief of Staff Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Bernard W. Aronson* Assistant Secretary of State for Inter-American Affairs William T. Pryce, Senior Director, Latin American Affairs, NSC (Notetaker) Eliza Burham, Interpreter Jean-Bertrand Aristide, President of Haiti Jean Casimir, Ambassador to the United States Fritz Longchamps, Ambassador to the United Nations Raymond Valcin, Minister Counselor, Embassy of Haiti DATE, TIME October 4, 1991, 9:40 - 10:10 a.m., D.S.T. AND PLACE: Oval Office The President: Welcome. They were asking questions on what the American people are interested about (use of force to restore the government of Haiti) and I gave the answer. We want to see democracy restored. I am anxious to see what you think about the situation. Is there something additional that we should do? Please let me know what you think. I would like to have you speak completely freely. ~ I am convinced that people want to live in democracy and that they will die for democracy. That is why we are happy with what you have said and done. We are alive with you in your hope for democracy. ~ On behalf of the Haitia

pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME November 15, 1991, 11:17 - 11:29am EST AND PLACE: Oval Office
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: 8398 THE WHITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telcon with Carlos Andres Perez, President of Venezuela on November 15, 1991 (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President Carlos Andres Perez, President of Venezuela Interpreter: Stephanie Van Reigersberg Notetaker: William T. Pryce/Robert B. Morley DATE, TIME November 15, 1991, 11:17 - 11:29am EST AND PLACE: Oval Office The President: Hello, how are you my friend? (U) President Perez: I am fine. It is a joy to hear your voice again. (U) The President: I am delighted to hear from you again. How are things going? (U) President Perez: There are a lot of problems, but I am optimistic. We both are accused of travelling too much. (U) President Bush: I agree with you there. (U) President Perez: I talked to a New York Times correspondent the other day. I argued that if ever anything was justified, it was President Bush's trips. I told the correspondent that you would be criticized by other countries if you did not travel. (U) President Bush: Good. That's the way I feel. I am going to do the Asia trip. I'm just trying to get Congress out of town first. There ,re just too many things going on that need attention. je> President Perez: Great. I called you on the following points. First, on Haiti, the report of Ramirez Ocampo was a good one. I am calling Aristide, encouraging him to accept what the Commission says in that rep

pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME March 13, 1989, 2:00 p.m. - 2:30 p.m. AND PLACE: Oval Office
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: <SEGRE'l' 8EGRE~ 1633 THE WHITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Meeting with Foreign Minister Arens of Israel PARTICIPANTS: United States The President Secretary of State James A. Baker, III John Sununu, The Chief of Staff to the President Brent Scowcroft, The Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Marlin Fitzwater, The Press Secretary -Ambassador William Brown, US Ambassador to Israel Paul Hare, Acting Assistant Secretary of State (NEA) Richard N. Haass, Senior Director National Security Council (notetaker) Israel Foreign Minister Moshe Arens Israeli Ambassador Moshe Arad Sallai Meridor, Policy Advisor DATE, TIME March 13, 1989, 2:00 p.m. - 2:30 p.m. AND PLACE: Oval Office Foreign Minister Arens began the meeting by noting that this March 26 marked the tenth anniversary of the Israel-Egypt Peace Treaty. He stated his hope that the event could be commemorated by a ceremony involving Prime Minister Shamir and Egyptian President Mubarak. Such a ceremony would also provide a reminder of the lack of diplomatic progress achieved since. The President said that his role in any ceremony would be affected by his schedule. He added that the real question was how to negotiate progress. His hope was that PM Shamir would corne to this country with new ideas. This would be important given the climate in the United States and his desire to move the peace

Lebras, WHSSS (Notetaker) DATE, TIME March 22, 1989, 2:43 p.m. - 2:48 p.m. AND PLACE: Oval Office - Cristiani's home The President, after an initial exchange of greetings, indicated that he did not wish to delay his congratulatory call any longer, even though the election results had not yet been certified. He asked Cristiani when he thought the certification would occur. <I) Mr. Cristiani responded that the certification had taken place about a half hour before. ¢> The President replied that his call was even more timely then. ~> The President congratulated Cristiani on his wonderful win, saying that the United States supports the government and people of El Salvador in their efforts to build democracy. The United States also understands and applauds EI Salvador's efforts to defend its society against terrorism. The President said that his call had two purposes, to congratulate Cristiani, and secondly, to welcome Cristiani's commitment to democracy. <~ Mr. Cristiani thanked the President and stated that the objectives of the United States were also those. of EI Salvador. pdfExtract.subject: SUBJECT: One-on-One Meeting with Prime Minister Shamir of Israel
pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: United States The President Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs, Notetaker Israel Prime .Minister Yitzhak Shamir Elyakim Rubinst , Cabinet Secretary
pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME Thursday, April 6, 11:00-11:45 a.m. AND PLACE: Oval Office
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: 2283 ADD ON 3 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: One-on-One Meeting with Prime Minister Shamir of Israel PARTICIPANTS: United States The President Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs, Notetaker Israel Prime .Minister Yitzhak Shamir Elyakim Rubinst , Cabinet Secretary DATE, TIME Thursday, April 6, 11:00-11:45 a.m. AND PLACE: Oval Office The President began by saying he liked one-on-one sessions because we could get more done than was possible in a plenary, and could remove misunderstandings. He expressed his delight that the Prime Minister was here, and made it clear that he was prepared to talk about a full range of subjects, whatever the Prime Minister wished. He explained that this small meeting would be followed by a plenary session, then public statements, an outing to the Air and Space Museum, and dinner, before which they could meet for an additional moment to discuss anything. The Prime Minister thanked the President for inviting him and for the opportunity to meet. His talks with Secretary Baker were encouraging. He hoped to make progress and believed we were going in the right way. Taking out his notes, Prime Minister Shamir observed that the President was a promoter of the program of strategic cooperation. He said that the program was going well and that he hoped the two countries could go even further

pdfExtract.declassification: DECLASSIFIED €OnFIDEN'fI}rL PER E.Oa 12958, Declassify on: OADR
pdfExtract.subject: SUBJECT: The President's Telephone Call to Salvadoran President Jose Napoleon Duarte
pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: The President David A. Pacelli, NSC (Notetaker) President Duarte The President began by saying, "Hello, Mr. President." President Duarte, speaking in English, said hello and asked how the President was. The President said he was fine, but he was interested in how President Duarte was. President Duarte said he was well. The doctors told him that the cancer was the same as before [before it began to grow again last month) . The President said that he was sorry to miss seeing President Duarte this trip. He was out of town, at Camp David. But he wanted to call up and pay his respects. President Duarte said, "Thank you." The President said that he realized that this may be the last trip President Duarte was making to Washington as President of El Salvador, and he just ~anted to pay his respects. President Duarte said he would come back in June for longer treatments. The President said that perhaps he would see him then. In any event, the President said, he wanted to pay his respects and also extend his condolences over the murder of the Salvadoran Attorney General and over the attacks on other government officials. The President said it was his impression that terrorist attacks by the FMLN on government officials were getting worse. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: THE WHITE HOUSE eOHFIB"KHT1M 3557 WASH I NGTON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Phone Call from President Bush to President Arias of Costa Rica re Panama PARTICIPANTS: The President Paul Lebras, Notetaker Jacqueline Murphy, Stenographer President Arias DATE, TIME May 11, 1989, 3:35 p.m. - 4:00 p.m. AND PLACE: The White House, Oval Office After an initial exchange of greetings, The President stated that he was calling regarding the troubled situation ~n Panama. He said that he had been in touch with President Perez of Venezuela and hoped that some common statement could be made, and he wanted to touch base and see if President Arias had any new thoughts or views on the situation. President Arias stated that he had been in touch with almost everyone, including Felipe Gonzalez of Spain. Arias said that he is trying to call a meeting of the OAS Foreign Ministers next Monday. He also stated that he had talked to President Perez this morning, along with some Central American countries. He had also offered to call Cerezo and Azcona, but not Nicaragua since they did not seem to ~e interested. He also stated that it was his understanding that Mexico also did not want to become involved. The President then verified that the OAS meeting would be held on Monday. President Arias replied in the affirmative, and The President urged that "the meeting be held as quickly as possible

Miller, Jr., NSC (notetaker) President Sir Dawda Jawara , Ousman A. Sallah, Gambian Ambassador to the United States Omar Sey, Minister of External Affairs Abdou Janha, Secretary General of the President's Office DATE, TIME, May 15, 1989, 10:00 - 10:10 am AND PLACE Oval Office The President welcomed President Jawara to the Oval Office. While the press corps was in attendance, the President kept the conversation on the weather and asked President Jawara about his talk on agricultural affairs at Cornell. President Jawara indicated that he spoke for about 50 minutes not only on issues of veterinary medicine but on broader issues affecting Africa. President Jawara pointed out to the President that The Gambia has a center for agricultural and veterinarian research. After the departure of the press, the President took the opportunity to welcome President Jawara to the White House and indicated his great personal support for the President's leadership of The Gambia. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: I:. -€9H~IDENTL"IL 4497 ADD-ON 2 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHI NGTON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Mohammad Hosni Mubarak of Egypt (~ PARTICIPANTS: The President Mohammad Hosni Mubarak, President Notetaker: C. David Welch, NSC DATE, TIME June 9, 1989, 1:20 p.m. - 1:26 p.m. EDT AND PLACE: Oval Office After opening pleasantries, the President offered a brief readout of the visit of Prime Minister Bhutto of Pakistan. He stated that relations were good with Pakistan; he was impressed by Bhutto. PM Bhutto was firm on Afghanistan in their talks. There had been a frank discussion of Pakistan's nuclear program. PM Bhutto had asked for help in bringing India to accept a - non-proliferation treaty. The President said that he had called Prime Minister Rajiv Gandhi afterwards, but it did not seem that there was that much Indian interest in such arrangements. However, Indian-Pakistani relations are better, and there is some chance that the two will meet again soon. (~) President Mubarak said that Gandhi is a good man. He said that he had advised the two leaders to keep in close touch and that he also had advised Gandhi to visit Pakistan. (~ The President asked how the Casablanca Summit had gone. (U) One, thank you very much for your assistance and for your letter about the situation in Jordan. I hope we can count on help and support not only from the u.S. but from our Arab colleagues. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: COHFIDEN'fIAL< CONFrDENTIAL· 6040 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHI NGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with the Pope, August 1, 1989; 4:13-4:25 p.m. PARTICIPANTS: The President The Pope Notetaker: Sandra Charles DATE, TIME August 1, 1989, 4:13 p.m. - 4:25 p.m. AND PLACE: The Oval Office The President began the conversation by apologizing for calling His Holiness so late and saying that he wanted to discuss the situation in Lebanon and the hostages. He stated that the United States was in a state of shock and sadness over the death of Colonel Higgins, who was serving with the United Nations in Lebanon but in fact was a U.S. citizen. The American people were shocked to the core. (U) The President then suggested a statement or a word from the Vatican would encourage those who did it to-return his body. Since the Pope was well respected and committed to peace, the President thought his intervention in this regard would be helpful with Hizbollah. (U) The Pope indicated that his first concern was the suffering in Lebanon. (U) The President acknowledged that but said if the Vatican could join with the UN to ask that the body be returned to the family, it would mean a lot to the American people. He noted that the UN Secretary General "had just sent an envoy to get the body returned. (U) . The President said that it was the question of the body, of when he was killed,

pdfExtract.participants: Participants: The President King Hassan II of Morocco Location: Oval Office Stenographer: Patty Daniel Interpreter: Alec Toumayan Notetaker: Ralph Sigler After initial pleasantries, the President told King Hassan that he was calling to discuss both the situation in Lebanon, and the Higgins' case, which had shaken America to the core. The President noted that he had issued a statement condemning all hostage-taking and abductions and calling for the immediate release of all hostages being held by any party. The President said he had talked to a number of world leaders . concerning this problem, and was appealing to the world to use whatever influence anyone has to obtain the return of the body of Colonel Higgins. He also said he had appealed to the Iranians, but was not hopeful of any help in view of the state of relations between the US and Iran. He also noted the UN Secretary General had been helpful and had sent a special emissary to the area. King Hassan said he fully shared the President's indignation and emotions and said he would help wherever possible. The President expressed his appreciation ~nd ~sked if he ~ould turn to the situation in Lebanon. King Hassan interrupted, saying he wanted to say a few more words on the hostage problem. He noted that the Arab leaders gathered at Casablanca had discussed the need for withdrawal of the Iranian Hizballah faction from Lebanon. He s.aid Syrian President Assad "behind closed doors" had given his personal pledge to the other heads of state that this was Syria's problem and he would take care of it. The President acknowledged Hassan's statement and noted that the US had seen some evidence of Syrian efforts to be helpful. King Hassan reminded the President, however, that in this business, Syria often takes with one hand what it gives with the other. The President expressed appreciation for anything King Hassan might be able to do. He then moved to the problem of Lebanon and the Arab initiative, saying he did not know exactly what the US might be able to do to be helpful, but he was willing to try if the King had any suggestions. The President said he had talked with the Pope yesterday and with King Fahd and Mrs. Thatcher and would be talking to President Bendjedid later today.

pageCountBasis: measured from online PDF with pdfinfo
sourceNote: Source: George H.W. Bush Library, Bush Presidential Records, National Security Council, Presidential Telcon Files, [Memorandum of Telephone Conversations (Telcons) - August 1989-December 1989]: August 1989. Confidential.

pdfExtract.declassification: DECLASSIFIED PER E.Og 12958, Declassify on: OADR
pdfExtract.subject: SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with King Fahd of Saudi Arabia
pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: The President King Fahd Notetaker: Sandra Charles
pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME, August 2, 1989, 10:33 a.m. 10:43 a.m. AND PLACE: The Oval Office
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: COUP IDlJWF lAb CONFIDENTIAL 6058 THE WH ITE HOUSE DECLASSIFIED WASHl NGTON PER E.Og 12958, AS AMENDED ~ooc - O'faCJ-F MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION 'Jt- 8'/~ I/(J, SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with King Fahd of Saudi Arabia PARTICIPANTS: The President King Fahd Notetaker: Sandra Charles DATE, TIME, August 2, 1989, 10:33 a.m. 10:43 a.m. AND PLACE: The Oval Office The President began by saying he wanted to call to discuss the situation in Lebanon with the hostages and more generally as well. (U) The President said that the world was up in arms about LTC Higgins. We assumed he was dead and didn't know for sure, and didn't know when he died. He knew the King didn't have any influence over the radicals, but wanted to make the same appeal he had made to the Holy Father, the British Prime Minister and head of the United Nations. The President suggested that anything the King could do to influence the return of the body of LTC Higgins would be greatly appreciated. He continued that it was brutal to keep the body there when the man's family was suffering. He told the King we had made a strong appeal to Iran through the Swiss, but we were not very hopeful there. We were asking anyone with any influence with Iran to see what they could do. We had condemned all hostage-taking and called for the immediate release of all hostages. Lastly, he mentioned the larger hostage situation.

(/) On the death of LTC Higgins, the President appealed to President Bendjedid on a humanitarian basis, asking if he had any ideas on how the body, which was under the UN banner, could be returned to his family. He noted the Syrians, who could be difficult, seemed to be cooperating on this matter. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: . . .€-Q~l¥IDg}TTIAL -€8NROENTlAl 6097 THE WHITE HOUSE WASH INGTON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBu-reCT: Telephone Call to Chancellor Helmut Kohl of West Germany (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President Helmut Kohl, Chancellor Notetaker: Robert Hutchings DATE, TIME August 3, 1989, 2:01 - 2:10 pm EDT AND PLACE: The Oval Office The President initiated the call and inquired about Chancellor Kohl's vacation. (U) Chancellor Kohl replied that it was too cool in the Austrian Alps but that he had been swimming, hiking and thinking. He had been thinking in particular of the President and sympathizing with what must be a terrible burden. This type of terrorism [in Lebanon] is one of the worst scourges mankind has experienced. The Chancellor said that he had seen on television pictures of the kidnapped Americans, which brought to mind the kidnapping and murder of his friend Hans Martin Schleier. Schleier had conveyed a message via television to the Chancellor during that period, so the Chancellor knew.what must be going on in the President's mind now. (U) The President expressed his very great thanks and said he wanted to bring the Chancellor up to date on the situation. The tape of Cicippio is of particular concern. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: ...." '111 'Yl.' J I '01 -EOtqf'IE'JEHIIAL . 6081 ADD-ON THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with Sultan Qaboos of Oman PARTICIPANTS: The President Sultan Qaboos Notetaker: Sandra Charles DATE, TIME, AND August 3, 1989, 11:31 - 11:42 EST AND PLACE: The Oval Office The President asked the Sultan how he was. Sultan Oaboos responded that he was fine. The President said that he had not talked to the Sultan in a long time and that he was delighted this call had worked out. Sulta~ QaboQ$ then said how sho9ked they were to hear of LTC Higgins' murder. The President replied that thi~ was one reason for his call. This tragedy had shaken our nation to the very core. LTC Higgins was a man wearing a uniform and a UN representative. He relayed what Sheikh Mohammed of Bahrain had said yesterday when he met with him: that made it the world's business. Everybody had expressed universal concern over this brutality. Sultan Qaboos stated that he was really shocked, and that this was not good for international relations. The President said that we presumed LTC Higgins was dead, but we didn't know for sure and we didn't know when he died, if he died. We were appealing to everybody -- Prime Minister Thatcher, the Pope, and others -- for the return of his body. He asked if there was any possible channel the Sultan could use to encourage, directly or

COWFIRi>NTIAL -bUNI-IULN IIAt 6226 THE WH ITE HOUSE DECLASSIFIED WASHI NGTON PER E.O. 12958, AS AMENDED ;\000 -()q~q-r MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION "JL a/~1 /fJ' SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with Mr. Cornelio Somrnaruga, International Commission - Red Cross PARTICIPANTS: The President Mr. Cornelio Somrnaruga Notetaker: Sandra Charles DATE, TIME, AND August 4, 1989, 4:00 p.m. - 4:10 p.m. PLACE: The Oval Office The President began, saying he was calling to say that he had seen references to the Red Cross involving a possible useful role for that organization on the hostage question. He said there were some references coming out of Iran and he didn't know if Mr. Somrnaruga had seen them or if there was a useful role for the organization. He wanted to bring them to his attention and encourage him to look into it, if it was something he felt comfortable with. (7) .- Mr. Sommaruga responded, . first by expressing his sympathy for LTC Higgins and all those suffering in these moments. He said the JCRC would be interested and would respond if they were certain all parties concerned requested them to work as a neutral intermediary. That was not the case now. He acknowledged there were some signals from the press that they might playa role and he assured the President that they would do so as soon as they were requested to. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: .·-BECRE'1'- 6328 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHINGTON DECLASSIFIED PER E.O. 12958, AS AMENDED ~6itJD -04;' ,-F MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION '1L 8/2 Ill), SUBJECT: Phone Call to the President from the Pakistani Prime Minister PARTICIPANTS: The President Prime Minister Bhutto Notetaker: Sandra Charles DATE, TIME, August 5, 1989, 10:43 p.m. - 10:53 p.m. AND PLACE: Camp David The Prime Minister began saying she was sorry to trouble the President but that he had said she should phone him if she had a problem. The reason she called was because she was having some difficulty with the President of Pakistan. Three months earlier he had threatened to directly abolish the Assembly. Without going into the details, she was calling the President to get his support for the democratic process in Pakistan and to restrain President Ghula;rn from carrying out his threat. (7) The President responded th'at of course we strongly supported the democratic process in Pakistan, but he really needed to know the details of the problem. (U) Prime Minister Bhutto said the problem had to do with the armed forces and the assignment of military officers. She explained that under the constitution the Pakistani President appointed officers and she retired them. L CONFIDENTIAL 6275 THEWH ITE HOUSE WASf-jINGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with - President Mubarak of Egypt (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President President Mubarak Notetaker: C. David Welch DATE, TIME, AND August 9, 1989, 2:06 p.m. - 2:15 p.m. PLACE: The Oval Office The President and President Mubarak exchanged greetings and conversed briefly about family matters. (U) The President said that he wanted to mention a couple of matters. He recalled his meeting in the spring with President Mubarak and the suggestion Mubarak had made that they stay in frequent touch. The President mentioned that he was therefore following the "Mubarak Plan", and had picked up the phone to call. (U) The President said that he would welcome any ideas on the hostage situation. He added that Defense Minister Abu Taleb had an excellent visit at the White House; he hoped that Abu Taleb had felt that it went well from his end. He continued that we had wanted to show Abu Taleb proper friendship and cooperation at both the White House- and at the Pentagon. Returning to hostages, the President said that he would appreciate any ideas, that he was prepared to explor~ any avenue- for the safe release of the hostages, and that he hoped someone in Egypt may have some infl uence . pdfExtract.subject: SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Mubarak of Egypt
pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: The President President Mubarak Notetaker: Stephanie Sullivan
pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME October 18, 1989; 1:17 p.m. - 1:22 p.m. AND PLACE: The Oval Office
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: 8426 THE WH ITE HO USE WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Mubarak of Egypt PARTICIPANTS: The President President Mubarak Notetaker: Stephanie Sullivan DATE, TIME October 18, 1989; 1:17 p.m. - 1:22 p.m. AND PLACE: The Oval Office President Mubarak: Hello. The President: How are you, Hosni? President Mubarak: How are you, George? The President: Fine, and you? President Mubarak: I was upset to hear about the disaster in San Francisco. The President: Yes, it is a disaster; it concerns us a lot. Fortunately, it is not as bad as it might have been. It is bad, but our people are responding really well. The whole city is involved in disaster-relief efforts. President Mubarak: Good, that's better, of course. The President: It's very nice of you to call. Brent Scowcroft said he talked with you earlier. Where are you, in Cairo? President Mubarak: In'Cairo. The President: How did the talks with Qadhafi go? President Mubarak: Very good. I'm going right now to brief Wisner. The President: Good. President Mubarak: The talks were encouraging. The President: Good. Tell Wisner to send me a copy of the brief. I'm very interested in how it went.

pdfExtract.declassification: SECRE'f' DECLASSIFIED Declassify on: OADR SE6RET PER EMO. 12958,
pdfExtract.subject: SUBJECT: Meeting with Defense Minister Yitzhak Rabin of Israel
pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Richard N. Haass, Senior Director for Near East and South Asian Affairs, NSC staff C. David Welch, Director for Near East and South Asian Affairs, NSC staff (Notetaker) Yitzhak Rabin, Israeli Defense Minister Moshe Arad, Israeli Ambassador to the U.S. Oded Eran, Minister, Israeli Embassy Avraham Ben Shoshan, Defense Attache BG Yekutiel Mor, Military Secretary
pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME January 17, 1990, 3:05 p.m. - 3:30 p.m. AND PLACE: General Scowcroft's Office Defense Minister Rabin: There are four points to be dealt with on the peace process: ($) First, there is the question of the composition of the Palestinian delegation. I am not aware of agreement even between the Palestinians and the Egyptians. No wisdom has 'been proposed so far. (1) Second, there is the issue of the agenda (for the dialogue) . Should it be real, leading to practical results? If it deals with the major issues, it will lead nowhere. (S) Third, who is to announce the Palestinian delegation and what they are comi~g for? ($> Fourth, who are the other participants in the talks? (t) Assurances are not really the focus now. After all, assurances were a U.S. idea. (i) Dr. Haass: You didn't have to take us up on this idea. ($) Defense Minister Rabin: We gave a positive response to the Baker five points and we are ready for a tripartite meeting. We hope to work out the details so this can proceed. ($) General Scowcroft: I hope so too -- I am getting pessimistic. (~) SECRE'f' DECLASSIFIED Declassify on: OADR SE6RET PER EMO. 12958, AS AMENDED ()1 6/2-I/oq A ___ --.. 1_ ;» __r-

Haass, Senior Director for Near East and South Asian Affairs, NSC staff C. David Welch, Director for Near East and South Asian Affairs, NSC staff (Notetaker) Yitzhak Rabin, Israeli Defense Minister Moshe Arad, Israeli Ambassador to the U.S. Oded Eran, Minister, Israeli Embassy Avraham Ben Shoshan, Defense Attache BG Yekutiel Mor, Military Secretary DATE, TIME January 17, 1990, 3:05 p.m. - 3:30 p.m. AND PLACE: General Scowcroft's Office Defense Minister Rabin: There are four points to be dealt with on the peace process: ($) First, there is the question of the composition of the Palestinian delegation. I am not aware of agreement even between the Palestinians and the Egyptians. No wisdom has 'been proposed so far. (1) Second, there is the issue of the agenda (for the dialogue) . Should it be real, leading to practical results? If it deals with the major issues, it will lead nowhere. (S) Third, who is to announce the Palestinian delegation and what they are comi~g for? pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: The President Prime Minister Shamir Sandra Charles, NSC Staff, Notetaker
pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME February 5, 1990; 10:48 - 10:51 a.m. EST AND PLACE: The Oval Office
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: 0919 ADD ON THE WHITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMCON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with Prime Minister Shamir of Israel PARTICIPANTS: The President Prime Minister Shamir Sandra Charles, NSC Staff, Notetaker DATE, TIME February 5, 1990; 10:48 - 10:51 a.m. EST AND PLACE: The Oval Office The President telephoned Prime Minister Shamir concerning the terrorist bombing incident on Sunday, February 4, 1990. The President: Mr. Prime Minister, how are you? Prime Minister Shamir: Fine. Thank you for calling. How are you? The President: I know this is a difficult day for you, but I just wanted to phone to say how sickened and saddened we are over the terrible news of what took place to the tourists in Egypt. It is a tragedy. I wanted to express my condolences to the families of the victims and to all Israelis who must be deeply shocked by this. Prime Minister Shamir: Thank you. I will convey your feelings to the families. It is really very painful. The President: This kind of cowardly attack demonstrates that we must be making progress toward peace or we wouldn't have these attacks, and those who 'oppose peace are in a minority and are forced to take these desparate actions. I am trying to put a positive spin on that. The only way to honor those who have sacrificed and to frustrate those who commit such crimes is to go forward on the path of peace. I want y

pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: 3024 Add On THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMCON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with King Hussein of Jordan (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President King Hussein Notetaker: C. David Welch DATE, TIME April 23, 1990, 1:17-1:22 p.m. AND PLACE: The Oval Office DECLASSIFIED PER E.O. 12958, The President: Hello, Your Majesty. (U) AS AMENDED ClOOIJ -o'1aQ-F ) King Hussein: Sir, how are you? (U) Jt- f( 1~llo7 The President: I'm just fine. Simply called to express my thanks to you for the kindness you showed to Senator Dole and Secretary Dole and the rest of that travelling group. They came back very enthusiastic and very grateful .to you. (U) King Hussein: Thank you very much. It was a very, very great pleasure to meet them and have frank and open discussions, and we enjoyed having them very much. I hope it was useful. (U) The President: They enjoyed it and as Senator Dole said in public about his trip -- it seems to have helped ease the tension. I was fascinated about their visit to Iraq. We're telling Iraq that they themselves must work to create an environment in which relations can be improved. Very frankly, the recent events have hurt, but we want to stay in touch. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: bUPiFIUEI~ IIJ.\L COHFIDEtil'IAt 3169 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHI NGTON MEMCON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Hafiz aI-Assad of Syria (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President President Hafiz aI-Assad Notetaker: David Welch Interpreter: Shukran Kamal DATE, TIME April 23, 1990, 1:31 - 1:59 p.m. AND PLACE: The Oval Office President Bush initiated the call. The President: Mr. President, I'm George Bush. How are you? (U) President Assad: Greetings to you, Mr. President. Thank you very much. How are you? (U) President Bush: Mr. President, let me say first of all that I am pleased to have this opportunity to talk to you personally. You're very kind to take my call, and I hope that in the future you will not hesitate to call me if there is something you would like to' discuss. (U) President Assad: Thank you. I'm very happy to talk with you. I will speak to you in the future if there are matters that would be helped if we were to talk together. (U) President Bush: It is good that our first conversation comes at a moment of joy for Robert Polhill and his family. Mr. President, I want you to know how sincerely thankful I am for your personal efforts for the release of Mr. Polhill. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: -eONFI9~N'fIlrlr GrIU~',i-,i b.C}i{ II ~l""l~t 3569 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMCON MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Mubarak of Egypt PARTICIPANTS: The President President Mubarak Richard N. Haass, NSC, Notetaker DATE, TIME May 4, 1990; 10:29 - 10:36 a.m. EST AND PLACE: The Oval Office The President initiated the call to President Hosni Mubarak. (U) The President: Hello, Hosni. I hear it's your birthday and I wanted to call and wish you a happy birthday and wish you all the best. Wish I were as young as you. (U) President Mubarak: Thank you very much. (U) The President: Listen, I was just checking in with you and following your travel. I just got your recent letter and I'll get back to you formally on that. But I was interested in wishing you a happy birthday and wanted to stay in touch and would like you to share your assessment of Assad. The guy's been helpful on the hostages and I wondered if you had talked to him. (U) President Mubarak: I spoke to him about the hostages and he promised he's doing the maximum. If he rushes them, they may kill them. He's doing as much as he can. (1) The President: He's dealing in good faith and I told him that. ~) President Mubarak: He told me and he's very pleased and doing every effort. (U) The President: And the rest of your visit went okay? Looks like you moved him in the peace process

pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: .. - - .;''-'" ..... ~ , 3879 THE WH ITE HOUSE WAS 1-(1 NGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Meeting with President Ben Ali of Tunisia (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Shuckran Kamal, Interpreter President Ben Ali Abdelaziz Hamzaoui, Tunisian Ambassador to the U.S. Afif Garbouj, Interpreter DATE, TIME May 15, 1990, 10:30 - 11:00 a.m. EST AND PLACE: The Oval Office The President: with your permission, let's start in here briefly, if you have any subjects you would like to discuss in private. (U) President Ben Ali: I would like to express my pleasure at being here and continuing our talks, especially since the United States enjoys such prestige and since our two countries agree on democracy, human rights, and economic progress. We can discuss other issues in our general meeting, but if you have any questions on our internal situation? ~ The President: We have great respect for the way you have been moving. We are concerned about stability in Algeria. It is so important that Tunisia continue its role of a stable element in the region. We have no sense of instability in Tunisia, but stability in the Maghreb as a whole is of concern to us and is very important. pdfExtract.participants: PARTICIPANTS: The President The Vice President Lawrence S. Eagleburger, Acting Secretary of State John H. Sununu, Chief of Staff Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Ambassador Robert Pelletreau, U.S. Ambassador to Tunisia John Kelly, Assistant Secretary of State, Near East Affairs C. David Welch, NSC, Notetaker Shuckran Kamal, Interpreter President Ben Ali Mohamed El Jari, Minister and Director of the President's Office Ismail Khelil, Minister of Foreign Affairs Mouldi Zouaoui, Secretary of State for Industry and Commerce Abdelaziz Hamzaoui, Tunisian Ambassador to the U.S. Salah Baccari, Advisor to the President Jamaledine Chichti, Advisor to the President Afif Garbouj, Interpreter
pdfExtract.dateTimePlace: DATE, TIME May ~5, 1990, 11:00 - 11:45 a.m. EST AND PLACE: Cabinet Room
pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: --,....... ..., ... -. 3879 THE WH ITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMORANDUM OF CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Plenary Meeting with President Ben Ali of Tunisia (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President The Vice President Lawrence S. Eagleburger, Acting Secretary of State John H. Sununu, Chief of Staff Brent Scowcroft, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Ambassador Robert Pelletreau, U.S. Ambassador to Tunisia John Kelly, Assistant Secretary of State, Near East Affairs C. David Welch, NSC, Notetaker Shuckran Kamal, Interpreter President Ben Ali Mohamed El Jari, Minister and Director of the President's Office Ismail Khelil, Minister of Foreign Affairs Mouldi Zouaoui, Secretary of State for Industry and Commerce Abdelaziz Hamzaoui, Tunisian Ambassador to the U.S. Salah Baccari, Advisor to the President Jamaledine Chichti, Advisor to the President Afif Garbouj, Interpreter DATE, TIME May ~5, 1990, 11:00 - 11:45 a.m. EST AND PLACE: Cabinet Room The President: This is a pleasant opportunity to renew the dialogue we started last November. We touched upon one or two matters in the Oval Office. We will have a little time before dinner to talk further. I have great respect for what you are doing in the area of democracy and economic reform. This will benefit Tunisia. So, welcome again. The floor is yours. Let me say that your statement outside was well-considered and well- received. (U) DECLASS

Gates, Assistant to the President for National Security Affairs Donald P. Gregg, U.S. Ambassador to Korea Richard Solomon, Assistant Secretary of State for East Asian and Pacific Affairs Douglas H. Paal, Director of Asian Affairs, NSC, Notetaker Chang Lee, Interpreter Roh Tae Woo, President Choi Ho -Joong, Minister of Foreign Affairs Park Tong Jin, Ambassador of the Republic of Korea Ro Jae Bong, Secretary General to the President Kim Chong Hwi, Assistant to the President for Foreign and National Security Affairs Roe Chang Hee, Senior Protocol Secretary, Interpreter and Notetaker DATE, TIME June 6, 1990 I · 10: 00 - 11: 00 AND PLACE: The Oval Office The President welcomed President Roh to the White House and the Oval Office. David Welch, NSC, Notetaker DATE, TIME June 8, 1990; 10:33 - 1042 a.m. EST AND PLACE: Iowa President Mubarak initiated the call; the President, traveling in Iowa, was patched through by White House Signal. (~ The President: Hello, Hosni, how are you? (U) President Mubarak: Hello, George, how are you? (U) The President: I'll bet I know what you're calling about. Well, I'm worried about the same thing, the dialogue with the PLO. But I'm delighted to hear from you -- I followed your role in Baghdad, and I want to tell you how much I appreciated it. (U) President Mubarak: I sent you a message two hours ago outlining the situation. Did you receive it? (~) The President: No, I diqn't see it, but I am in Iowa. When did you send it? (~ President Mubarak: I sent it about four hours ago. Please read it. It is regarding the U.S.-PLO dialogue. It is very important to keep it (the dialogue) now; if it is cut, it will create a hell of a problem. «() The President: I realize that but there's another side. pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: €OHFIDEN3?IhL 5000 ADD ON THE WHITE HOUSE WASH 1 NGTO N MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with King Hussein of Jordan PARTICIPANTS: The President King Hussein John Montgomery, Notetaker DATE, TIME June 23, 1990, 7:36 - 7:42 a.m. AND PLACE: Camp David The President initiated the call to King Hussein to thank him for his birthday message and to discuss the Middle East Peace proces s . ()2') . The President: Hello, Your Majesty? (U) King Hussein: Good morning, how are you? (U) The President: Well, I am all right. I am calling on a couple of matters. The first one is very brief and pleasant. Thank you for your thoughtful birthday greeting. I passed this 66-year old mark. It was a very nice greeting. (U) King Hussein: Thank you, Mr. President and I hope you have many, many more happy and productive years. (U) The President: I am very lucky here. I am calling you on your recent call to our Ambassador about the PLO. Our Ambassador sent in a very factual report explaining that this is a matter that complicates your life and that troubles me very much. I wanted to tell you from our standpoint, given the predicament that it started, it was very difficult to continue status quo. I had to go forward to let the PLO know they must reiterate their position that Arafat opposes this terrorism against another country. Let me make two points. We have not cut off

pdfExtract.firstPageSnippet: -~ET 4999 ADD ON THE WHITE HOUSE WASHINGTON MEMCO~ MEMORANDUM OF TELEPHONE CONVERSATION SUBJECT: Telephone Conversation with President Mubarak of Egypt (U) PARTICIPANTS: The President President Mubarak John Montgomery, Notetaker DATE, TIME June 23, 1990, 8:10 - 8:16 a.m. AND PLACE: Camp David The President telephoned President Mubarak to discuss the Middle East Peace process. (¢') The President: Hello, Hosni? (U) President Mubarak: Hi, George, how are you? (U) The President: All right. I am calling you on the recent difficulty and wanted to talk to you about a minute if you have a minute here. We heard from Frank Wisner about your concern and disappointment and let me start by saying I do understand this. I hope you know, given the history, I could not act otherwise as the talks are predicated on a renouncement of terrorism. I left the door open by not ending the talks, but just suspending them. I am willing to resume talks if Arafat and the National Council do what we asked. It has to happen. Arafat must simply say this attack is not good and here's the action taken to do something about it. Having left it at that, I hope the Palestinians will not reject out of hand talking to Israel and turn to violence. I just don't know where to go.
